app.title: Hipótesis
controls.title: Control Strategies
controls.description: Enable and configure population control measures to see their projected effect.
controls.lethal.label: Lethal Control
controls.lethal.description: Humane euthanasia applied by wildlife specialists. The only method modeled to achieve population reduction at scale.
controls.sterilization.label: Sterilization
controls.sterilization.description: Surgical sterilization of captured individuals. Logistically intensive: each animal requires weeks of preparation.
controls.sterilization.note.males: Note: sterilizing males has negligible effect while fertile males remain in the population. Female sterilization drives the birth rate reduction.
controls.displacement.label: Displacement
controls.displacement.description: Relocation to zoos or sanctuaries. Limited by worldwide capacity and complex CITES quarantine requirements.
chart.title: Hippo Population Projection
chart.xAxis.label: Year
chart.yAxis.label: Population
stats.tooltip.currentPopulation: Population estimates disagree: official counts put the 2023 population near 169 (Cornare) rising to ~200 by 2026, while the fitted model of Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023, Scientific Reports) gives ~91 in 2022 / ~131 in 2026. This app uses the official counts.
stats.tooltip.populationIn2060: Estimated number of individuals in 2060 under the currently selected control strategies.
stats.tooltip.yearsToCapacity: Year the population is projected to cross 1,500 individuals. This is a milestone, not an ecological carrying capacity: Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023, Scientific Reports) model growth as density-independent (~9.6%/yr) and find no indication that hippos approach a carrying capacity in the coming decades — the Magdalena basin could physically hold tens of thousands.
stats.tooltip.projectionHippoYears: Total area under the projection curve (2026–2060). Each unit represents the environmental impact of one individual present for one full year — an aggregate measure of the total impact of the invasive process over the analyzed period.
waste.title: Ecological footprint
waste.subtitle: Cumulative dung these hippos would produce over 2026–2060, shown filling Hacienda Nápoles (~22 km²) — the estate they escaped from. The outline is a schematic size reference, not a precise boundary.
waste.figureAlt: Schematic outline of Hacienda Nápoles filling with dung emojis in proportion to the waste generated.
waste.tooltip.mass: Cumulative wet dung by mass, from the area under the projection curve (hippo-years) × ~10 kg per hippo per day (wet mass; Stears et al. 2018, PNAS).
waste.tooltip.pools: The same waste by volume, expressed in standard Olympic swimming pools (2,500 m³ each), assuming a water-like density of ~1,000 kg/m³.
waste.tooltip.area: Ground area the waste would cover if spread uniformly 1 cm deep. Hacienda Nápoles — the estate the hippos escaped from — covers roughly 22 km².
waste.nitrogen: Tonnes of nitrogen loaded to water
waste.tooltip.grass: Cumulative terrestrial grass grazed, from hippo-years × ~45 kg per hippo per night (Stears et al. 2018, PNAS — the midpoint of 40–50 kg). Colombian hippos are assumed to eat like African ones; this has not been measured locally. Football-pitch equivalent assumes ~2 kg of fresh grass per m² of pasture.
waste.tooltip.nitrogen: Cumulative nitrogen carried into water via dung and urine, from hippo-years × ~45 kg N per hippo per year (derived from Subalusky et al. 2015). This is a loading rate, not a volume of water 'contaminated' — it drives eutrophication, cyanobacteria blooms and oxygen crashes, as documented in the lakes near Hacienda Nápoles (Shurin et al. 2020, Ecology).
about.title: About Hipótesis
about.intro: Hipótesis is an open educational tool that visualizes population growth projections for the invasive hippopotamus population in Colombia's Magdalena river basin. The model is based on peer-reviewed ecological research.
about.model.title: Population Model
about.model.description: The historical curve (1981–2026) is not simulated: it interpolates documented census estimates, which disagree — official counts reach ~200 by 2026, while the fitted model of Castelblanco-Martínez et al. (2023) gives ~131. This app uses the official counts. From 2026 the projection uses a density-independent, sex-structured birth model at ~9.6%/year net growth, matching that same study, which found no indication the hippos approach a carrying capacity in the coming decades (the Magdalena basin could physically hold tens of thousands). Growth is therefore exponential — the 1,500 line on the chart is a mid-century milestone, not an ecological ceiling — and control measures are what bend the curve.
about.impact.title: Environmental impact estimates
about.impact.description: The ecological-footprint figures scale the area under the projected population curve (hippo-years) by per-hippo rates from the literature. Waste assumes ~10 kg of wet dung per hippo per day, shown spread 1 cm deep over Hacienda Nápoles (~22 km²), the estate the hippos escaped from. Grass grazed uses ~45 kg of vegetation per night, expressed as football pitches assuming ~2 kg of fresh grass per m². The water figure is nitrogen delivered (~45 kg N per hippo per year) — a loading rate that drives eutrophication, not a volume of water 'contaminated'. Rates are measured on African hippos and assumed to apply to the Colombian population, which has not been independently measured.
about.references.title: References
about.references.castelblanco2021: Castelblanco-Martínez, D.N., et al. (2021). A hippo in the room: Predicting the persistence and dispersion of an invasive mega-vertebrate in Colombia, South America. Biological Conservation, 253, 108923.
about.references.stears2018: Stears, K., McCauley, D.J., Finlay, J.C., et al. (2018). Effects of the hippopotamus on the chemistry and ecology of a changing watershed. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 115(22).
about.references.subalusky2015: Subalusky, A.L., Dutton, C.L., Rosi-Marshall, E.J., & Post, D.M. (2015). The hippopotamus conveyor belt: vectors of carbon and nutrients from terrestrial grasslands to aquatic systems in sub-Saharan Africa. Freshwater Biology, 60(3).
about.references.dutton2018: Dutton, C.L., Subalusky, A.L., Hamilton, S.K., Rosi, E.J., & Post, D.M. (2018). Organic matter loading by hippopotami causes subsidy overload resulting in downstream hypoxia and fish kills. Nature Communications, 9, 1951.
about.references.shurin2020: Shurin, J.B., Aranguren-Riaño, N., Duque Negro, D., et al. (2020). Ecosystem effects of the world's largest invasive animal. Ecology, 101(5), e02991.
about.references.data: Boundary data: Colombian department and river polygons from open GeoJSON datasets (DANE). The Hacienda Nápoles waste-reference outline is a schematic footprint near the real estate location; its ~22 km² area is the load-bearing figure, not the drawn shape. Reference values: Olympic pool (2,500 m³) and FIFA football pitch (105 × 68 m) by definition.
footer.disclaimer: Population projections are estimates based on a simplified mathematical model. They are intended for educational purposes and do not constitute official scientific advice.
map.title: Spatial distribution
